speaker: interviewer
text: Such a good doggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that at the end, your sores were dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't tap you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Yes, especially the one on my left foot. Very, very because we had nothing-- I mean, there was a salve, not to cure, but to at least clean it out. But if you didn't get to a hospital or a [INAUDIBLE], you didn't have it. And you didn't want to broadcast it either that you had this. I mean, you didn't want to tell people you had it. You don't. You just don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this time, I was in the camps. Except for this, which became very bad, I wasn't sick. I couldn't afford to be. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] How should I say this? I trained my mind not to feel the things I couldn't afford to feel. And when it became too bad, I let my mind go back home, knowing that I'm taking time out.

speaker: interviewee
text: While I'm standing, freezing, barefoot for no reason at all-- because Himmler was supposed to come to take a look at the beautiful camp and decided not to come. We were put out and the Russians to stand for 12 hours on Christmas day. It was the largest Christmas tree you have ever seen in the world. All for him. For PR work, propaganda. And he didn't show, so they took us out. Just for fun. For 12 hours. And you stand barefoot, no underwear. Just the prison dress. So these are the moments when you must let your mind get out back home, where it's warm, another Christmas, another people. There's a danger you don't get it back into you anymore. So these are things you have to practice and you have to learn. True, when it's over, your body is stiff, and frozen, and you need somebody to help you to get back in again. But while you're standing there and your mind is somewhere else, you don't feel the cold. You don't feel the cold. You don't feel that your feet are frozen, or that you have sores which hurt very much. It's a matter of really training. And this sounds crazy, but, in a way, I think this is Gandhi did. Certain pains you can't survive otherwise. Or hunger. The hunger becomes so severe. You know, everybody talks about food. It's insane. The more you talk about food, the hungrier you get. Don't talk about food. But you have visions of these things. Of course, of course. The other thing which is very important-- in Auschwitz, impossible, I must say. But let's say in Ravensbruck. Some of the situations are so bizarre, you've got to laugh about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You can't take it serious that you're sleeping next to ax murderers. It's insane. Or that somebody would ask you whether you play bridge and is deadly serious about it. Why is he asking that? Not because there are cards to play. She's deciding the class distinction. She's a very fancy lady-- was-- who had a very fancy shop in Czechoslovakia. Lady's clothes. Very fancy. And she wants to know whether I'm good enough to be considered a friend of hers, talk about it. It's insane. You can't take this serious. You've got to laugh about these things. However, you need somebody. You need a strong friend there. You need a buddy there who, when it comes to how much longer? 3:30 in the morning, do I have to get up? There's somebody there who helps you a little bit and visa versa. You learn to sleep while you're standing up, while they try to count you, which they don't know anyway. You learn to sleep while you march. You learn things which do not make-- I'm standing there already for six hours. How much longer? You sleep. How were the food and the conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food? They had the same chef that they had in Auschwitz. They went to the same cooking school. I have no doubt about it. No food. The [NON-ENGLISH] stole here, as much there, as much as they did in Auschwitz. And when you sang and they liked what you did, they came, and gave you a little bit of this, and a little bit of this. A bit of garlic, being on top of the list. And toilet paper. And a bit of soap. And then later on, you were allowed to get packages once a month, which, of course, half of it was stolen. And whatever wasn't stolen-- I mean, chocolate was on the undergarment. So you didn't have-- whatever it was. No, you were always hungry. You were always hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they allow you to grow your hair back there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three inches. Three inches you were allowed because, you see, they said it is for cleanliness because then lice can't come in. The problem is, they didn't talk about it to the lice. Not a single lice knew they weren't supposed to come in unless it was over three inches. They came in when it was one inch. What did they care? Not at all. The other thing was later on, they built factories outside the camp, on top of the hill. Siemens. It was Siemens for the war effort. And 2,000 of the inmates-- and I was one of them-- was marched up to Siemens and down for a while, every day, to do something for the Germans, for the war effort. They never paid us. It was slave labor for Siemens, big Siemens.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I sat. And they called it heissleiter. H-E-I-S-S-L-E-I-T-E-R. It was soldering, but it was platinum. So something terribly valuable. And it was not bigger than perhaps my thumb. And that had to bake in ovens, and it was, as I was told, via the grapevine, directly for Hitler's planes. A very hush, hush, hush, hush thing. A very hush up thing. And in this factory, where I was, it was mostly French, who sabotaged wonderfully. The civilians were Germans from Berlin. And we did the work and we had, of course, guards who were not allowed to interfere with the work itself. And that went on for 12 hours a day. And then they built our barracks up there also, so that we shouldn't have to walk up and down, but spend more time working, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now when there were already alarms for bombs, we were not allowed to leave the factory. The Germans left, but we were not allowed to leave. We had to stay. And we were often wondering whether they knew what was down there, the British fliers, or the French fliers, or the American fliers. And if they knew, would they bomb? Even so knowing that there are prisoners in there? We would have liked them to bomb, really. Much better than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now there was a spy in each one of the holes-- a prisoner. For a piece of bread, or a coat, or boots, they would sell out. What they were supposed to do is tell the Nazis who was doing what sabotage, of course. Now we knew we had a spy, but we didn't know who it was. We met a guy there-- an engineer-- they were all engineers, the civilians there-- who was in charge of inspection of all the holes. He turned out to be from the German underground. I think he was the only one. I never knew there was a German underground. He was it. By the name of Teddy. He looked like a teddy bear. He was looking for people, prisoners, who were gutsy enough or stupid enough to find out, through a set up network in the camp itself, who was really in the camp on political prisoners, on high political prisoners, like de Gaulle and some of the de Gaulle click. He got to this buddy of mine-- to Eva and me. And he checked us out very carefully before he dared to come to us. And Eva was from Czechoslovakia. And Ravensbruck had an awful lot of Czechs there. And they hated the Germans with a wonderful passion. I mean, really. And they went all out, trying to find out as much as they could. A lot of them worked in offices and things like this, so they had access to a lot of information.

speaker: interviewer
text: And for us giving him the information, he would tell us how the war went. We had no idea. Auschwitz? You had no idea. In Auschwitz, I don't know who knew how the war went. I did not. And in my group, nobody knew anything. But in Ravensbruck-- which is, by the way, only 80 kilometers from Berlin. So it was the last camp that was held, the last concentration camp, the last spot that was held. In Ravensbruck, they knew, and we wanted to know. So he gave us information about really went on with the war. So this worked fine for a while. And one day, Teddy disappeared. He was never heard from again. I have no idea what happened to him, if anything. And my buddy, Eva, and I were arrested. Sounds crazy, since we were already in a camp. We were arrested by the German Gestapo, who had a political office outside Ravensbruck, on top of a hill. I have no idea, geographically, where.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was not because they were worried about us, but for the names of the Germans who were running around in Berlin. So we were first in one cell. It was an underground bunker, right outside Ravensbruck, which we had heard existed, but didn't know. We had also heard, which was true, that when the first assassination of Hitler took place, which, unfortunately, didn't work, these officers were sitting in that bunker in Ravensbruck. So were all the big politicians from my city, Vienna. Karl Seitz, the mayor of Vienna, the democratic mayor. A lot of them. From Leipzig, there was the mayor of Leipzig. These political people were all sitting in Ravensbruck. And I think the reason was probably because it was so close to Berlin and it was supposed to be held anyway until the bitter end. So they weren't worried about us, but about the names, which we had in the German underground, which was working. Well, there, of course, it came to be very important that this was not my first day in prison because I may have told them anything they wanted to hear. After two years, you don't. You know the one thing you don't do-- you don't say anything. And the second thing is you know that what they want from you, if they have it-- bang, bang-- you're dead. There's no reason for them to keep you alive, if they already have what they want. So the only way to stay alive, if barely, is not to say the names. Then we were separated. And like Eva, I was alone in a cell, under ground, for three months. And that was rough. That was very rough because they had figured out-- they, the Germans-- scientifically, how long you can exist and live on certain rations. Namely, every fourth day a piece of bread and what they call coffee. You can exist for a long time except there, of course, your mental things start being a bit weird. You don't know what is right, and what is yes, and what is no. And then first, they turn the light off. You have no light at all. My window was way up there because I was way under ground. Then they take away the bunk. Then they take away the blankets. You're in an ice cold cell. Also, by the way, there's a spy. Always a spy there. Another prisoner in the cell. When you already come in, there's another prisoner there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, if you're new, you will say to them, why are you here? Well, I'm here because of such and such. After two years, you don't do this anymore. You don't know whether there's a spy or not, but you keep your mouth shut. I don't know why I'm here, which, by the way, I didn't. I really didn't at that time. When the spy doesn't work, the spy disappears after a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is hired to find out things. If she doesn't find it out, they don't take away her coat or the boots, but she's no better off than I. You don't know what happens to her either. Then they have these torture cells, which the spy introduced us to right away. There is the tread cell where you tread water. If you don't tread it, you drown.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is the cross. You're literally on a cross. I mean, after they take the food, and the light, and the cold away. And then there is one with a doctor next to you who will first examine you to make sure that you don't drop dead. How much can you take? Then they put you on a table down, face down, naked. There's a doctor. There is one with a whip. There is an assistant and a guard. You get whipped right across the kidney.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a pig skin. They're very proud to tell you this. It's a pig skin thing. The lashes-- 10, 15, 25. It depends how many you can take. They don't want you to drop dead or to have a heart attack because then again, you're no good to him. What else they do is the political office is hundreds and hundreds of crooked little steps way up on a hill. They call you for interrogation in the middle of the night. After you haven't eaten, after you don't know whether it's day, night, what month at all, a guard takes you out and you have to climb-- this is the middle of winter, I'm talking about. There's ice, snow, and it's very cold. Very cold. And you are certainly not dressed for it. Up those steps, which, toward the end, you can't. I mean, they drag you up. You no longer walk on your own. And up there is a little house. I have no idea where it is. If somebody would take me up there, I don't know. I know there is a house. I don't know what else is there. And in that house, there's a foyer and then there is that office of criminal kommissar, who used to be this criminal kommissar-- used to be the police president of Berlin in charge of vice. So he dealt only with prostitutes, and pimps, and crooks, and so on. And he has another of those nice, little pig skin things. He has a desk, which is huge. And on this desk, piles and piles of papers. The first time I came up there, what he does is this. He must have practiced. There's no other way. Two inches away from your face. It doesn't hit you, but you can feel the wind in all of it. He says, I am criminal kommissar. The name has gotten off-- criminal kommissar. Oh my god. It will come to me in a moment. A reddish man in his thirties. And he has the file in front of him. And then he already accuses you of everything. You have no idea what the heck he is talking about. The man looked at my file and said to me, ah, you come from Auschwitz. I'll never forget this-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] You must have jumped off the grave digger's shovel. And interesting, you come from Auschwitz-- as far as Ravensbruck is concerned, the guards as well as this creep, there is some kind of, she's going to be tough. And he said to me, now I know you're tough and you know that I know. OK. And he said to me-- it was about a six hour interrogation, always with this, and he never touched me-- I will call you back. It doesn't matter how long it takes, day or night, day and night, until I have the names I need. And you know you have no chance, if you just say one name. Expect that I didn't know as many as he thought I knew. Very few.

speaker: interviewer
text: These interrogations went on every three, four nights. I did not konw whether Eva was alive. I did not know what happened. They play also that stupid game-- good policemen, bad policeman. Your friend told us everything. She already confessed. Why don't you confess and make it easy? I mean, this is also works when you're in prison for a day, not after two years. You know that. I knew she would never talk. And she knew I would never talk. So it didn't work. But there came a time when I didn't know anymore whether I was awake or not, whether what I thought happened, really happened or not. It was a kind of a twilight thing. I thought, why can't I get pneumonia? Why don't I get pneumonia? How can I not get pneumonia? It's freezing. And your mind wanders. And you don't know whether you want your mind to wander anymore or whether it does anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know that the steps-- next time they call you, no way. There are more steps every time. It's the same steps. It just seems much more, more, more to climb. And you fall and the ice is there. And then you see there's like no footprints on the ice. Nobody will know that I've been here. Nobody will know I've ever been here. Not that I've walked up there, not that I've walked down, not how many times. You, yourself, lose track of how many times you've been up there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why, always, you ask the soldier what time it is? It's always 2 o'clock in the morning or 3 o'clock in the morning. Then there is one time when I hear knocking on my wall next to me. There's somebody next to me. And I'm trying to remember, from my girl scout days, the Morse code, so that I can signal. There's somebody alive next to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I can't remember the code anymore. And I don't know whether I can't remember because I've forgotten, because of those months without food and cold, or whether I've just forgotten because it's been so long. I just signal back. Then I know that above me is a floor full of German soldiers because, every time now, we have a lot of Russian planes flying over, they keep singing in German, hooray, welcome the Russian fliers. They come again, they come again. These are soldiers who no longer want to march with Hitler. In other words, they marched away. They marched the other direction. So they are put up there, in the bunker. But, of course, they have a much better life. Where I am, under ground, are all the political prisons. However, the big shots are all the way in front because when I walked out to get up to interrogation, I see cells all the way in front. And just toward the end, the cell doors are open. And I recognize, in one of them, my mayor, from New York-- an old man, sitting there with a book-- and the Mayor of Leipzig. Now they are brought over the staircase, so if they each have to evacuate, they're going to have them as pawns. They have those very important political prisoners to exchange against some of their own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the bunker altogether? No. No, because I never got all the way to the back. I know I was pretty far in the back because I know when they walked me out, it was a long hallway. Or perhaps it seemed long because I was not in very good shape. But a long hallway. And they were in front, so that they could get them out fast. That much, I know. And I know that just in the last interrogation, a different man was sitting there. I still can't think of this name. it will come to me. It was a different kommissar. A Dutch guy, a young, Dutch guy. And I thought, I can impress him by speaking Dutch. He didn't care a bit about it, but I also noticed he was not very, very, very professional in what he did. He was new in the job. Totally new. The next thing-- it was three months I was down there-- we were evacuated from the bunker. Out. The bunker was totally evacuated because, again, it was toward the end that they expected this camp to be taken over. They wanted the political prisoners to be taken away and safe somewhere. And Eva and I were sent back in the camp. And when I came out, after three months, there were wall to wall-- or I should say, camp street to camp street of people lying, prisoners lying. There were 100,000 people from all the camps, which had been already evacuated. Whoever could-- including Auschwitz, all the Hungarians from other camps-- they were walked, on trucks, whatever, to this one camp. Nobody had room for them. Nobody had food for them. Nobody knew what was going on. They were just lying around on the street. Or they were taken off the trucks, loaded on the trucks again, into a forest-- bang, bang, bang, bang. I remember one guy telling me, we just don't have room for all of them. It's strange, the things you remember. Not enough room. It was the last camp that was being held. I couldn't go anywhere either. They didn't have room anywhere. We hid for-- it must have been three weeks. Not, we hid. We didn't have a place. We didn't know it then-- in the crazy block. It was the only block which would let us in. They didn't want us anywhere because they were so full. They didn't have that much space. So the first one who let us in turned out to be the crazy block. And I'm talking about people who were not crazy at all when they came to Ravensbruck. One side of the camp were all the lesbians which had also stolen. I don't know whether they were in because they were lesbians, or because they'd stolen, or both. The other side, not of the camp, but of the block, were all crazies. Now I didn't know this. I woke up in the morning, and my clothes were gone. The one I wore. It was pouring rain. And the one the [NON-ENGLISH] gave me to change-- nothing was there anymore. This is the block which the Nazis over there used for their own entertainment. They would come every morning-- it was kind of fenced in-- and start the show.

speaker: interviewer
text: The crazy one was the one who stole everybody's clothes because somebody had told her that if she has six times the sweater and six times the underpants, she can get home. So she stole everybody's clothes at night and put them on. And they had her parade in front of them. And what's in there? This isn't six. This is only five or four. You've got to have six. And she became a wonderful thief. She stole right under-- while I was asleep. It was gone the next day.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they had a race. They were tying their arms and their legs. And they had to hop to the end of the block. And whoever made it first without falling-- what did they say-- gets sausage and sauerkraut. Sausage and sauerkraut for whoever makes it first. And then they had a wrestling match. And the more you screamed, the better it was. And the more you kicked the other, the better it was. Again, whoever won got sausage with sauerkraut.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then they had a little Polish girl. They told her, if she finds the right shoes, then she can walk home, then they will let her go home. She was from Poland. And since Poland is very, very, very far, she needs good shoes. So this little girl didn't steal. She would walk, every day, from one to other, and said, please, can I have your shoes? They said if I have the right shoes, I can walk home. High heels, low heels, too big, too small.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day, again, when she came out, they said, no, no, no. These are not the right shoes. Pretty shoes for a pretty girl, try again. And this went on every day. And then one day, we got from the front office, where they knew me because of my singing-- get out of there. The same day, they packed them all together and out into the woods. Bang, bang, bang. And that was it. Then we went from one block to the next. And the rumors started that Count Bernadotte of Sweden was coming to take us out. Us is Dutch, French, Belgian women. 700 every day until all of them are empty. Somebody in the front office said she didn't want to be there at the end of the war because the Russians were coming in, 100,000 women were coming out. They're going to kill thousands. They'll be trampled to death. It's going to be a madhouse. This is no time to be there. You've got to get out there. A lot of the German women, who really lived in Berlin or very close, walked. They just walked out. They wanted to walk home. That was the time when the German officers and then the guards started taking off their clothes and putting on prison clothes. This is when a lot of them fled, a lot of them stayed. Now very important is one uber [SPEAKING GERMAN], even though there was a commandant and the gestapo, she really ran the camp. 24-year-old, blonde, blue-eyed, with a little dog. She's the one who made lamp shapes out of human skin. She was into pigmentation. Pretty lamp shades for a pretty girl. She fed, she kicked, she shot. And her hobby was-- this is a historical fact-- lamp shades.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as you see, it was not Auschwitz with gas chambers, but there was, in the forest, from the first day on, we all could hear shooting at night. And we didn't know. It was not target practicing. They took people out for whatever reason and just shot them. Now I know that the fact is that when I was up to the interrogation-- I know it was up there somewhere. But nobody knew. We just heard it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Long after the war, when the Nuremberg trials came up and some British officers followed me to Switzerland, wanting to have testimony about Ravensbruck, about specific people who killed specific people, this came out. There was never a witness to the shootings, the killings that went on in Ravensbruck, up there, in the forests.
